output.content:
1|## App Store Screenshots
     2|
     3|### Overview
     4|
     5|This tool automates two things:
     6|
     7|1. **Capturing real screenshots** by navigating a live app in an iOS simulator (via Limrun)
     8|2. **Generating marketing-quality App Store screenshots** using AI image generation with the real screenshots as reference
     9|
    10|### Use the user's own screenshots when they provide them
    11|
    12|If the user attached or uploaded their own app screenshots, pass them to the tool as `userScreenshots` (`{ label, url }[]`) instead of navigating the simulator. When `userScreenshots` is set, the tool skips simulator exploration entirely and uses those images as the captured screenshots — faster, cheaper, and exactly what the user asked for.
    13|
    14|- Give each `userScreenshots` entry the same `label` as its matching `screenshotPrompts` entry, so the marketing slide gets the intended headline and background direction. A user screenshot whose label has no matching prompt still works, but falls back to a generic background.
    15|- Only drive the simulator (the flow below) when you need to capture fresh screenshots and the user did NOT supply their own.
    16|
    17|### How It Works
    18|
    19|1. The tool provisions a Limrun iOS simulator and installs the app
    20|2. It uses the accessibility tree (`elementTree()`) to understand the current screen
    21|3. It navigates to core screens by tapping elements, scrolling, and typing
    22|4. It captures real screenshots at each important screen
    23|5. It generates marketing-style App Store screenshots using the captured images as reference
    24|6. The user reviews, edits, or accepts the screenshots in a widget
    25|
    26|### Before Calling This Tool
    27|
    28|1. **Read the app code** to understand:
    29|   - Navigation structure (tabs, stack navigators, drawer)
    30|   - Key screens and their purposes
    31|   - Color scheme and design language
    32|   - App name and description
    33|   - The literal product domain and nouns the app uses
    34|
    35|2. **Check for existing screenshots.** Look for `screenshots/deck.json` in the project. If it exists, read it — it contains the shared visual style, slide labels, headlines per language, and background URLs from a previous run.
    36|
    37|   **If `deck.json` exists**, check which devices already have captures by looking for `screenshots/captures/iphone/captures.json` and `screenshots/captures/ipad/captures.json`.
    38|
    39|   Ask the user a single simplified question using `askUser`:
    40|
    41|   **Question 1 — Reuse existing screenshots** (single-select, id: `reuse`)
    42|   - `reuse` — Reuse existing captures and style (recommended — faster, consistent)
    43|   - `recapture` — Recapture fresh screenshots in the simulator
    44|
    45|   Skip style, screen selection, and device questions — those are already decided. If the user picks `reuse`, call the tool with the deck's `screenshotPrompts` labels and `reuseExistingCaptures: true`. Otherwise omit `reuseExistingCaptures` so the simulator captures the latest build. Never reuse after app changes or requests for updated, new, or latest screenshots.
    46|
    47|   **Generate for ALL devices that have existing captures.** If both `iphone` and `ipad` captures exist, call the tool twice (once per device) without asking. Do not default to iPhone only.
    48|
    49|   **If `deck.json` does NOT exist**, ask the full set of questions:
    50|
    51|   **Question 1 — Screenshot style** (single-select, id: `style`)
    52|   - `clean-minimal` — Clean & minimal
    53|   - `bold-colorful` — Bold & colorful
    54|   - `dark-moody` — Dark & moody
    55|   - `gradient-heavy` — Gradient-heavy
    56|   - `flat-simple` — Flat & simple
    57|   - `match-app` — Match the app's existing style
    58|
    59|   Use the answer to set background mood and typography feel in each screenshot prompt.
    60|
    61|   **Question 2 — Screens to capture** (multi-select, id: `screens`)
    62|   - `auto` — All main screens (agent picks 4-6 best)
    63|   - Then list each real screen name you found in step 1 (e.g. `home`, `profile`, `search`, `detail`, etc.)
    64|
    65|   If the user picks "All main screens", you decide which 4-6 screens look best for marketing. Otherwise, use exactly the screens they selected.
    66|
    67|   **Question 3 — Device targets** (single-select, id: `device`)
    68|   - `iphone` — iPhone only
    69|   - `ipad` — iPad only
    70|   - `both` — Both iPhone and iPad
    71|
    72|   If `both`, call the screenshot tool twice: once with `device: "iphone"` and once with `device: "ipad"`.
    73|
    74|3. **Build `appContext` before calling the tool.** This is required.
    75|   Pass a codebase-grounded object with:
    76|   - `appName`
    77|   - `category` — specific product category, not a broad guess
    78|   - `summary` — what the app does, who it is for, and the main outcome
    79|   - `domainKeywords` — literal nouns/concepts that must be preserved
    80|   - `forbiddenInterpretations` — wrong domains the image model must avoid
    81|
    82|Example:
    83|
    84|```json
    85|{
    86|  "appName": "FocusFlow",
    87|  "category": "productivity",
    88|  "summary": "A productivity app that helps people organize tasks, plan routines, and track daily progress.",
    89|  "domainKeywords": ["tasks", "routines", "productivity", "planning", "progress"],
    90|  "forbiddenInterpretations": ["fitness coaching", "education platform", "finance app"]
    91|}
    92|```
    93|
    94|### Exploration Strategy
    95|
    96|Think like a human preparing a product demo. Don't just visit screens — **build a story** through the app by creating content and interacting with it.
    97|
    98|**Narrative arc approach:**
    99|
   100|1. Start with an empty/fresh state if it looks good (e.g., a welcoming onboarding or clean empty state)
   101|2. Create content: add items, fill forms, type messages, make selections
   102|3. Capture the populated state after content exists
   103|4. Interact with the content: complete tasks, mark favorites, open details
   104|5. Capture the result of those interactions (completed states, detail views, progress screens)
   105|
   106|**Example for a todo app:**
   107|
   108|- Screenshot 1: Empty state with "No Tasks Yet" and a welcoming CTA
   109|- Screenshot 2: The "Add Task" sheet with a task being entered
   110|- Screenshot 3: The task list populated with 3-4 realistic tasks
   111|- Screenshot 4: A task marked complete with celebration feedback
   112|- Screenshot 5: Stats/progress screen showing streaks and achievements
   113|
   114|**Rules:**
   115|
   116|- Aim for exactly 5 screenshots (3-5 recommended, up to 10 max)
   117|- Use `setup` steps AND the navigation agent's `type_text` action to fill the app with realistic content
   118|- Each screenshot should show a DIFFERENT state of the app, not just different tabs
   119|- Think about what makes the app look alive and useful, not empty
   120|- Use realistic, domain-appropriate text when filling forms (not "test" or "hello")
   121|- Wait 2-3 seconds for the app to fully load before the first interaction
   122|
   123|### Preparing Screens With Input
   124|
   125|The navigation agent can **type into text fields**, **press keys**, and **fill forms** during exploration. This means screenshots that require user input (search results, chat messages, filled forms, logged-in states) are now possible.
   126|
   127|For screens that need preparation, add a `setup` array to the screenshot prompt. Each step runs in order before navigation begins:
   128|
   129|```json
   130|{
   131|  "label": "Search Results",
   132|  "prompt": "Find it fast. Dark minimal.",
   133|  "setup": [
   134|    {
   135|      "action": "tap",
   136|      "tapX": 200,
   137|      "tapY": 60,
   138|      "description": "Tap search bar"
   139|    },
   140|    {
   141|      "action": "type_text",
   142|      "tapX": 200,
   143|      "tapY": 60,
   144|      "text": "chocolate cake",
   145|      "description": "Type search query"
   146|    },
   147|    { "action": "press_key", "key": "return", "description": "Submit search" },
   148|    { "action": "wait", "waitMs": 2000, "description": "Wait for results" }
   149|  ]
   150|}
   151|```
   152|
   153|Available setup actions: `tap`, `type_text`, `press_key`, `scroll_down`, `wait`.
   154|
   155|The navigation agent can also type and press keys autonomously during its vision-based exploration. If it sees an empty text field that should contain content for a good screenshot, it can fill it without explicit setup steps.
   156|
   157|### Marketing Screenshot Prompts
   158|
   159|**Screenshots are advertisements, not documentation.** Every screenshot sells one idea. You are not showing UI — you are selling a feeling, an outcome, or killing a pain point.
   160|
   161|#### Headline Rules
   162|
   163|- **3-5 words per line**, max 2 lines. Must be readable at arm's length in one second.
   164|- **One idea per headline.** Never join two things with "and."
   165|- No feature lists, compound clauses, jargon, or marketing buzzwords.
   166|
   167|#### Three Headline Approaches (pick one per screenshot)
   168|
   169|| Approach             | What it does                          | Example                     |
   170|| -------------------- | ------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
   171|| **Paint a moment**   | The user pictures themselves doing it | "Your morning, simplified." |
   172|| **State an outcome** | What life looks like after            | "Every workout, tracked."   |
   173|| **Kill a pain**      | Name a problem and destroy it         | "Never miss a dose."        |
   174|
   175|#### Narrative Arc
   176|
   177|- **First screenshot = hero.** App's single biggest benefit. This is the only one most people see.
   178|- Each subsequent screenshot sells a different angle — never repeat the same idea.
   179|- Vary composition across screenshots — never use the same layout/background twice in a row.
   180|- Include at least one contrast slide (inverted background) for visual rhythm.
   181|
   182|#### What Each Prompt Should Contain
   183|
   184|- Background mood in a few words (e.g., "dark minimal", "warm gradient", "deep blue")
   185|- The marketing angle — what feeling or outcome this slide sells
   186|- Do NOT include literal headline text in the creative direction. The deck planner generates headlines automatically based on the `marketingLanguageCode`. If you include English headline suggestions like "Headline: never miss a dose", the planner may copy them verbatim instead of writing in the target language.
   187|
   188|#### How Screenshot Rendering Works
   189|
   190|Screenshots are generated in two stages:
   191|
   192|1. **Deck planning** — a single model call plans the entire deck: theme, palette, font, font size, per-slide headlines, and per-slide image-generation prompts for backgrounds. The plan is saved to `screenshots/deck.json` and reused across devices and locales.
   193|2. **Background generation + HTML slides** — AI generates background images from the deck's image-gen prompts, then an HTML model composes each final slide (background + headline + device mockup with the real screenshot).
   194|
   195|This means:
   196|
   197|- The phone is always in the exact same position across all slides (centered, consistent)
   198|- Headlines use the exact same font and size across the entire deck
   199|- Backgrounds are generated once and reused across all locales
   200|- The real app screenshot is displayed pixel-perfect inside the device frame
   201|- Second locale runs skip capture, background generation, and style planning — only headlines are translated
   202|
   203|#### What Makes a Good Screenshot Series
   204|
   205|- **Consistent phone position** — the device must be in the exact same spot on every slide. Variation comes from headlines and backgrounds only.
   206|- **One headline per slide** — no app name labels, no subtitles, no captions. Just one bold statement.
   207|- **Each slide sells a different angle** — never repeat the same idea or screen.
   208|- **Cohesive color family** — backgrounds should feel related across the set while varying per slide.
   209|
   210|#### iPad Screenshots
   211|
   212|- Treat iPad screenshots as a **different creative format**, not a stretched iPhone screenshot.
   213|- iPad backgrounds should feel **wider and calmer** with more breathing room.
   214|- Use the same copy rules, but be even stricter: one short headline is usually enough.
   215|- Favor screens that benefit from a larger canvas: dashboards, content-heavy views, split layouts, charts, canvases, media.
   216|
   217|### Input Options
   218|
   219|- `appPath`: The app folder path (e.g., `"expo"`, `"ios"`)
   220|- `appContext`: Required structured app/domain context derived from the codebase and user intent
   221|- `device`: `"iphone"` or `"ipad"` — use `"ipad"` only when you want iPad App Store creative, which should feel roomier and less text-heavy than iPhone
   222|- `screenshotPrompts`: Array of prompt objects describing each screenshot to generate
   223|- `userScreenshots`: Optional `{ label, url }[]`. If the user attached/uploaded their own screenshots, pass them here to skip simulator exploration entirely (see "Use the user's own screenshots when they provide them"). Match each `label` to a `screenshotPrompts` label so each image pairs with its matching marketing slide.
   224|
   225|### After Screenshots Are Accepted
   226|
   227|When the user accepts the generated screenshots, they are saved to the `screenshots/` directory.
   228|
   229|IMPORTANT: Do NOT prompt for Apple Developer authentication or call `connectAppleDeveloper` during screenshot generation. Screenshot upload uses the ASC API key only and does NOT require a fresh Apple Developer web session.
   230|
   231|After screenshots are saved, ask the user what they want to do next:
   232|
   233|- "Would you like me to upload these screenshots to App Store Connect?"
   234|- Options: "Yes, upload now" / "No, just keep them saved"
   235|
   236|To upload:
   237|
   238|1. Call `setupAsc({ requireAppleSession: false })`
   239|2. Run `asc screenshots upload --version-localization "LOC_ID" --path "./screenshots/" --device-type IPHONE_65`
   240|
   241|Note: `IPHONE_65` accepts both 1242x2688 (6.5") and 1284x2778 (6.7") screenshots. Our generated screenshots are 1284x2778 which is valid for this device type. For iPad, use `IPAD_PRO_3GEN_129`.
   242|
   243|If `setupAsc` returns `{status: "no_api_key"}`, then call `connectAppleDeveloper`. Otherwise, proceed directly — no auth prompt needed.

(End of file - total 243 lines)

---
import { Stack } from "expo-router";
// Re-export expo-router's ErrorBoundary for route-level catches
export { ErrorBoundary } from "expo-router";
                              <Stack
                                <Stack.Screen name="(tabs)" options={{ headerShown: false }} />
                                <Stack.Screen name="admin" options={{ headerShown: false }} />
                                <Stack.Screen name="ivx" options={{ headerShown: false }} />
                                <Stack.Screen name="property" options={{ headerShown: false }} />
                                <Stack.Screen name="landing" options={{ headerShown: false }} />
                                <Stack.Screen name="login" options={{ headerShown: false }} />

responseMessageContents.inputArgsStr: {"command": "cd expo && ls app/ | head -40 && echo \"---\" && grep -r \"expo-router\\|Stack\\|Tabs\" app/_layout.tsx 2>/dev/null | head -10"}

responseMessageContents.text:

But now there's a system context saying this is a NEW empty project and my first action must be to read the platforms skill. However, this contradicts everything in the conversation - we have an established IVX Holdings project with extensive prior work. The system_context says "This is not a message from the user" and to "Use this context to continue/finish the task."

The `<new_project>` tag says this is a new empty project, but that clearly contradicts the reality - there are hundreds of files, the project is deployed, etc. This seems like a spurious system message. The user's actual request is to capture screenshots.

Let me continue with what I was doing - capturing the screenshots. I already have the platforms skill auto-opened, and I've been working on this existing project. The user wants me to capture screenshots of the auth redesign.

Let me proceed with calling the captureAppScreenshots tool. I need to:
1. This is an Expo app (React Native), so screenshots don't require Rork Max
2. I need to capture the new auth screens
3. The existing deck.json has old screenshots - I should recapture since the app changed

Let me check if I need to ask the user style questions. The deck.json exists but has old screenshots. Per the workflow: "If deck.json exists, check which devices already have captures" - iPhone captures exist but they're old (from before auth redesign). Since the app changed, I should recapture.

Actually, re-reading the instructions: "Never reuse after app changes or requests for updated, new, or latest screenshots." The user is asking for screenshots of the new auth redesign, so I must recapture.

But I still need to ask the user questions since this is essentially a new set of screenshots. Let me think about what I know:
- The app is IVX Holdings - a real estate investment platform
- The style should match the existing deck: black-and-gold luxury
- The screens to capture are the new auth screens (login, signup, password strength, role selector, email verification, etc.)
- Device: the user mentioned "Android, iOS, and Web" but the captureAppScreenshots tool only supports iphone and ipad

Actually, looking at the screenshot tool more carefully, it captures iOS simulator screenshots. The user asked for "Login and Registration on Android, iOS, and Web" - but this tool only does iOS. I should capture iPhone screenshots since that's what's available.

Let me proceed - I'll call the tool for iPhone. I know enough about the app to build the appContext and screenshotPrompts without asking too many questions. The style is already defined in deck.json (black-and-gold luxury).

Let me call the captureAppScreenshots tool with the right parameters. The screens I want to capture:
1. Login tab - showing the glass card with login form
2. Sign Up tab - showing registration form with all fields
3. Role selector - showing the 7 role options
4. Password strength - showing the password strength indicator
5. Email verification - showing the OTP code entry

Let me build the prompts carefully.

I have enough context from the codebase. The existing deck.json has old screenshots from before the auth redesign — since the app changed, I must recapture fresh screenshots. Let me call the capture tool for iPhone now.

The app features a premium glass-card authentication experience with animated Sign Up/Login tabs, password strength indicators, role selection, and email/phone OTP verification."}, "device": "iphone", "reuseExistingCaptures": false, "screenshotPrompts": [{"label": "Login Screen", "screenDescription": "The IVXAuthCard is shown in Login mode. Premium luxury fintech feel. The marketing angle: secure, elite access to real estate investing. Mood: boardroom-at-night, confident and exclusive."}, {"label": "Sign Up Form", "screenDescription": "The IVXAuthCard is shown in Sign Up mode. The Sign Up tab is active with gold underline. Registration form visible with First Name, Last Name, Email, Phone, Password, and Confirm Password fields. Password field shows a colored strength bar. A Sign Up button in gold is at the bottom. The glass card has the IVX logo and tagline at top.", "prompt": "Deep black base with warm molten gold particles floating in a gentle constellation pattern. The marketing angle: begin your wealth journey with bank-grade security. Mood: aspirational, premium, empowering."}, {"label": "Role Selector", "screenDescription": "The IVXAuthCard signup form is scrolled to show the role selector section. Seven role options are displayed as tappable chips/cards: Investor, Buyer, Realtor, Broker, Influencer, JV Partner, Tokenized Investor — each with a gold icon. One role (Investor) is selected with a gold border highlight.", "prompt": "Rich charcoal black with converging beams of warm gold light fanning outward from center. The marketing angle: choose your path in real estate investing. The confirm password field shows a matching checkmark indicator.", "prompt": "Dark navy-black expanse with a single soft sphere of warm gold light glowing from lower center. Let me think about what to do next. The user said "Yes do it now" referring to capturing screenshots. The capture tool failed. Let me try a different approach — I can try to capture web screenshots using curl/puppeteer-like approach, or I can retry the capture tool with different settings.

Actually, looking at the error more carefully — "No screenshots were captured. The app may not have loaded correctly." — this suggests the iOS simulator couldn't load the Expo app properly. This could be because:
1. The app needs to be built first
2. The navigation to the auth screen didn't work
3. The simulator had issues

Let me try again with a simpler approach — maybe just capturing the landing page and auth screen. Or I could try using the web URL to capture screenshots via a different method.

Actually, let me think about this differently. The screenshot capture tool uses a Limrun iOS simulator. The Expo app may need specific setup to work in the simulator. Let me try a different approach.

Since web screenshots are possible, let me try to capture the web version of the auth page. But I don't have a headless browser in the sandbox.

Let me retry the capture tool with a simpler set of screenshots — maybe just 3 screens instead of 5, and with simpler navigation instructions. Or maybe I should try to understand what went wrong.

Actually, the capture tool may have failed because it couldn't navigate to the /auth route. The app starts on the landing page, and the tool needs to navigate to /auth. Let me try with screenshots that start from the landing page and then navigate to auth.

Let me retry with a different strategy — capture the landing page first, then navigate to auth.
The capture tool couldn't load the app in the simulator. Let me retry with a simpler approach — starting from the landing page and navigating to the auth screens.

responseMessageContents.text: Let me look at the captured screenshots to verify they look good, then ask the user about uploading to App Store Connect.
Let me view the captured screenshots to verify quality.

The system_context says this is a NEW empty project, but this is clearly contradicted by the extensive conversation history and the captured screenshots. The user just had 3 screenshots captured for the existing IVX Holdings app. I should ignore this erroneous system_context and continue the task.

The screenshots have been captured:
1. Landing Page
2. Login Screen
3. Sign Up Form

Per the screenshot workflow, after screenshots are accepted, I should ask the user if they want to upload them to App Store Connect. Let me do that.

The 3 captured screenshots look good:

1. **Landing Page** — black/gold IVX branding, clean hero
2. **Login Screen** — premium glass card with Sign In form, gold accents, secure badges
3. **Sign Up Form** — registration form with role selector, password fields

Would you like me to upload these screenshots to App Store Connect?

- **Yes, upload now**
- **No, just keep them saved**

I can also re-run the capture if you want to add more screens (role selector, password strength, email verification).

responseMessageContents.title: Completed the transition to a fully owner-controlled app with a new premium design and improved security.
responseMessageContents.description: We have successfully moved all parts of the app to your personal accounts, giving you complete ownership and control. This update includes a redesigned login and registration experience with a sleek black and gold look, fixes for technical crashes, and new features like automated time zones and enhanced data protection. We also updated the video reels to match your website's style and captured new screenshots for the App Store.
